coredata.dc:description: Over the last decades, bacterial resistance has become one of the emerging health threats. Particularly dangerous are bacterial strains resistant to various antibacterial drugs. Herein, we modified graphene quantum dots (GQDs) to produce efficient photo-induced antibacterial agents. GQDs were modified with (a) ethylene-diamine (EDA), (b) with EDA and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs), and (c) 3-amino-1,2,4-triazole (TA) using carbodiimide coupling. Photo-induced antibacterial activity of modified GQDs was tested against 8 bacterial strains. Treatment with modified GQDs and blue light (wavelength of 470 nm) resulted in remarkable antibacterial activity with minimal inhibitory concentrations (MIC) of 7.81 µg mL−1 for K. pneumoniae and S. aureus and 3.9 µg mL−1 against MRSA and E. faecalis. Planar organization of GQDs functionalized with AuNPs allowed direct access of molecular oxygen to AuNPs leading to more efficient 1O2 production as well as the 1O2 production from excited GQDs. Thus, GQDs functionalized with AuNPs showed outstanding efficiency in the battle against several bacterial strains, particularly those that lead to nosocomial infections.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Over the last decades, bacterial resistance has become one of the emerging health threats. Particularly dangerous are bacterial strains resistant to various antibacterial drugs. Herein, we modified graphene quantum dots (GQDs) to produce efficient photo-induced antibacterial agents. GQDs were modified with (a) ethylene-diamine (EDA), (b) with EDA and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs), and (c) 3-amino-1,2,4-triazole (TA) using carbodiimide coupling. Photo-induced antibacterial activity of modified GQDs was tested against 8 bacterial strains. Treatment with modified GQDs and blue light (wavelength of 470 nm) resulted in remarkable antibacterial activity with minimal inhibitory concentrations (MIC) of 7.81 µg mL−1 for K. pneumoniae and S. aureus and 3.9 µg mL−1 against MRSA and E. faecalis. Planar organization of GQDs functionalized with AuNPs allowed direct access of molecular oxygen to AuNPs leading to more efficient 1O2 production as well as the 1O2 production from excited GQDs. Thus, GQDs functionalized with AuNPs showed outstanding efficiency in the battle against several bacterial strains, particularly those that lead to nosocomial infections.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the increase in bacterial resistance toward antibiotics [1], the requirement for novel strategies for preventing and treating bacterial infections is urgent. Among many pathogens, methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus epidermidis (MRSE) and methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA) are G+ bacterial strains with the highest clinical significance considering their resistance to various antimicrobial drugs [2–6]. As an alternative to traditional antimicrobial agents, numerous natural and synthetic nanomaterials have shown significant antibacterial activity [7–9].

Due to their biocompatibility, low production price, and antibacterial effects, carbon-based nanomaterials become one of the rising stars in the search for new antimicrobial treatments [10–13]. Carbon-based quantum dots such as graphene (GQDs) and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) showed antibacterial effects [14–17]. The mechanisms of GQDs antibacterial activity are related to the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) upon light illumination (photo-dynamic therapy – PDT) and induction of oxidative stress in bacterial cells in the Type I, and the Type II mechanisms [14,18]. GQDs also showed peroxidase-like activity and enhanced •OH formation from the H2O2 [19]. Dots induced a photothermal effect [20] as well as physical damage to the bacteria cells [21].

Surface chemistry plays a very important role in the antibacterial activity of carbon nanoparticles [8,22–24]. Bing et al. showed that the CQDs surface charge is a key factor in establishing initial interaction between bacterial cells and nanoparticles, verifying the internalization of positively charged dots within the negatively charged cell wall of Escherichia coli (E. coli) [25]. On the opposite, negatively charged S- and N-doped CQDs (-6.47 and −47.18 mV, respectively) showed higher antibacterial efficiency [17].

This paper explores the possibilities of surface chemistry modulation and investigates the photo-induced antibacterial activity of GQD to create an efficient, visible light-triggered bactericidal agent. Namely, as-produced GQDs are insufficient against bacterial cells [26]. By modification of GQD with ethylene diamine (EDA), positively charged groups are expected to improve the internalization of dots in bacterial cells, while combining them with 1,2,4-triazole, more efficient antibacterial activity is expected considering that its derivates are one of the new classes of antimicrobial agents [27]. Gold nanoparticles show limited antibacterial activity [28–30], but they increase the ability of photosensitizers to produce reactive oxygen species (ROS) [31].

Herein, for the first time complex nanoparticles with GQDs and AuNPs were created as efficient, visible light-induced, antibacterial PDT agents that have not yet been reported. Namely, GQDs were functionalized with a variety of organic amines using a combination of N-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-N′-ethylcarbodiimide (EDC) and N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) [32], or 4-dimethylaminopyridine (DMAP), EDC, and 1-Hydroxybenzotriazole (HOBt) [33]. Thus, GQDs functionalized with borondipyrromethene derivate through an EDC/NHS coupling reaction showed excellent potential for photodynamic cancer therapy [34]. A similar reaction was applied to bond peptide GE11 to GQDs to target nasopharyngeal carcinoma [35]. In our work, for the first time, a carbodiimide coupling reaction was used to attach AuNPs on amino-functionalized GQDs. To the best of our knowledge, these dots have not been investigated as antimicrobial agents, until now.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of GQDs, AuNPs, and modification of GQDs with selected amines
ce:section.ce:para: Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) were produced as described elsewhere by the electrochemical oxidation of graphite electrodes [36]. AuNPs were produced following the classical citrate synthesis method using chloroauric acid as a precursor [37]. In the electrochemical oxidation of graphite electrodes, 3 wt% dispersion of NaOH in EtOH 96 vol% was used as an electrolyte for the electrochemical reaction [38]. In the further text, these dots will be referenced as pristine or p-GQDs.

GQDs were modified using amines, ethylenediamine (EDA), and 3-amino-1,2,4-triazole (TA) by employing a carbodiimide-catalyzed reaction between carboxyl groups of GQDs and primary amino groups of two selected molecules.

GQDs powder (100 mg) was dispersed in 100 mL of demineralized water and placed in the ice bath. After 15 min, 478 mg of 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDCI, > 98.0 %, TCI Chemical Trading Co., Tokyo, Japan) was added under vigorous stirring. The pH was adjusted to 5 using 1 M HCl. Then, amine was added (1 mL EDA or 150 mg TA). The reaction mixture was stirred for 30 min on the ice bath followed by stirring at room temperature for 24 h. Then, the products were isolated using dialysis for one week (Spectra/Por® dialysis membrane MWCO of 3.5 kD). After, dispersions were collected and the concentrations were adjusted to 1 mg mL−1 using mild heating (at 80 °C). In the further text, GQDs functionalized with EDA will be referenced as GQDs-EDA, while those with TA as GQDs-TA.

To produce quantum dots functionalized with AuNPs, GQDs-EDA were used for EDCI coupling reaction with gold nanoparticles exposing COOH groups on their surface. To prepare GQDs modified with AuNPs, 12 mL of AuNPs were mixed with 111.2 mg of EDCI in the ice bath. Then pH was adjusted to 5, followed by 30 min of stirring to stabilize the reaction mixture. Then 40 mL of GQDs-EDA (concentration: 0.58 mg mL−1) was added and stirred at room temperature for 24 h. The sample was cleaned and dried as previously mentioned and named GQDs-EDA-AuNPs.

For UV-Vis spectroscopy, water dispersion of GQDs-EDA, GQDs-EDA-AuNPs, GQDs-TA, and AuNPs in the concentration of 0.08 mg mL−1 were prepared. Absorption spectra were obtained using an LLG-uniSPEC 2 Spectrophotometer (Lab Logistic group, Mekenheim, Germany) at 20 °C, in the air. Spectra were recorded in the wavelength range of 200–800 nm.

Fluorescence features of GQDs-EDA, GQDs-EDA-AuNPs, and GQDs-TA were examined by PL spectroscopy. PL spectra were recorded using the Horiba Jobin Yvon Fluoromax-4 spectrometer (Horiba, Kyoto, Japan). The excitation wavelengths were 300 nm, 320 nm, 340 nm, 360 nm, 380 nm, 400 nm, 420 nm, 440 nm, and 460 nm, while the emission spectra were recorded between 320 and 900 nm, depending on the excitation wavelength. For PL measurements, GQD samples were dispersed in demineralized water at a concentration of 0.25 mg mL−1. The excitation slit was 8 nm, emission slit was 2 nm. The integration time was 0.5 s. All spectra are collected at standard temperature (20 °C) and atmospheric pressure.

Both Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) and ζ-potential measurement were collected at an angle of 173° with a Zetasizer Nano ZS instrument (Malvern Panalytical, Malvern, United Kingdom) equipped with a 633 nm He–Ne laser. Measurements were performed at a fixed position (4.65 mm) with an automatic attenuator and controlled temperature (20 °C). Before analysis, GQDs (0.2 mg mL−1) were sonicated using a tip sonicator (Hielscher UP400St with 400 watts and 24 kHz, Teltow, Germany) for 30 min. The number-weighted diameter distribution was acquired to estimate the average particle size. 10 independent measurements were taken. Data were presented as a mean along with the 95 % confidence interval. Solvent-resistant micro cuvettes (ZEN0040, Malvern, Herrenberg, Germany) have been used for experiments with a sample volume of 40 μL. The ζ-potential of GQDs was calculated from the electrophoretic mobility using the Henry correction applied to Smoluchowski's equation, as implemented in the Zetasizer data analysis software. For this purpose, 5 independent measurements at pH 7 were averaged.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was conducted using a ULVAC-PHI PHI5000 VersaProbe II scanning microprobe (ULVAC-PHI, Chigasaki, Japan), operating with a monochromatic Al K α source (1486.36 eV). Samples were analyzed with a 100 μm ⌀ beam (25 W). For each sample, both survey scans (Pass Energy, PE = 117.000 eV, energy resolution 1.00 eV, 1100–0 eV) and high-resolution scans for O 1 s, N 1 s, C 1 s and Au 5 f regions (PE = 23.500 eV, energy resolution 0.05 eV) were collected. The collected electrons were analyzed in FAT mode. The samples were prepared by drop-casting deposition over aluminum foil; during the data acquisition, the sample surface was kept at an angle of 45° to the analyzer, and charge neutralization was used during all the experiments employing both electrons and positive ions (Ar⁺).

Scanning electron microscopy with energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (SEM-EDS) was performed on an INCAx-act LN2-free analytical silicon drift detector of characteristic X-rays with PentaFET® Precision and AZtec 4.3 software package (Oxford Instruments, UK) connected to a TESCAN Mira3 XMU (20 kV, SE detector). GQDs powders were deposited on the double-sided conductive, adhesive type and analyzed.

For Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR) analysis, powdered GQD samples were mixed with KBr powder and compressed into pellets. FTIR Spectrometer Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS20, Thermo Scientific™, Massachusetts, United States was used in the range of 4000–400 cm−1 at 32 scans per spectrum and a resolution of 4 cm−1.

The surface morphology of GQDs and GQDs-EDA-AuNPs samples was visualized by atomic force microscopy (AFM). We used an AC160 cantilever (MFP-3D Origin, Asylum Research, Oxford Instruments, Santa Barbara, California, United States), operated in tapping and viscoelastic mode at room temperature. For each sample, it was selected 20 AFM images and the nanoparticles’ size (diameter and height) was calculated in Gwyddion software [39].

Electrostatic force microscopy (EFM) measurements were performed at room temperature with an MFP 3D Origin (Asylum Research, Oxford Instruments, Santa Barbara, California, United States). A Si cantilever coated with Ti-Ir (ASYELEC-01) was used. A cantilever resonant frequency v0 was 75 KHz, a Q factor of 130, and a spring constant k of 2.8 N/m were measured using a thermal noise method. A tip voltage of 9 V was used.

Photo-induced production of singlet oxygen by functionalized GQDs was investigated by measuring the photobleaching of singlet-oxygen probe 9,10-anthracenediyl bis-(methylene)dimalonic acid (ABDA) [40] and 1,3-diphenilisobenzofuran (DPBF) [41] using UV-Vis [42]. The reaction between ABDA and 1O2 was followed by a decrease in the intensity of the absorption band at λ = 378 nm. Due to insolubility in water, ABDA was first dissolved in DMSO (4.1 mL), and then demineralized water was added (20 mL) so that the final probe concentration was 104 μM. Then, functionalized GQDs were mixed with the probe solution: 1.5 mL of water dispersion of GQDs in the concentration of 1 mg mL−1 was mixed with 1.5 mL of ABDA stock solution (or 1.5 mL demineralized water for reference). The mixtures were then exposed to a blue-illumination source (λ= 470 nm) located 15 cm from the upper sample surface and illuminated for 300 min, while absorption spectra were collected. As a control, ABDA was mixed with demineralized water to achieve the final concentration of 52 μM and exposed to illumination in the same condition as GQD samples. Absorption spectra were recorded from 0 to 270 min of illumination. DPBF was used to investigate the 1O2 photo generation as well. In this experiment, DPBF in the concentration of 0.26 mmol L−1 in ethanol 96 vol% was mixed with water dispersions of GQD samples in the concentration of 1 mg mL−1, in the volume ratio 1:1. The decomposition of DPBF was observed by lowering the absorption band intensity at 410 nm. DPBF solution (concentration 0.13 mmolL−1) without GQDs was a reference. Absorption spectra were recorded for 15 min using a LLG-uniSPEC 2 Spectrophotometer (Lab Logistic group, Mekenheim, Germany) at 20 °C, in air. The wavelength range was 200–600 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photoinduced antibacterial activity of GQDs - minimal inhibitory concentration assay
ce:section.ce:para: The minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) values were determined according to the standard broth micro-dilution assays, recommended by the National Committee for Clinical Laboratory Standards for Bacteria (M07). Antimicrobial activity was tested against 8 bacterial strains: Staphylococcus aureus NCTC 6571, S. aureus MRSA ATCC 43300, Enterococcus faecium ATCC 6057, Pseudomonas aeruginosa ATCC 10332, Klebsiella pneumonie ATCC BAA 2146, Listeria monocytogenes NCTC 11994, Escerichia coli NCTC 9001, and Acinetobacter baumanii ATCC 19606. Luria-Bertani broth was used to cultivate bacterial test organisms, and cultures were diluted to the concentration of 5×105 colony-forming units (CFU) mL−1 for bacteria. Dispersions of functionalized GQDs were serially diluted in media to the 500–3.91 μg mL−1 concentration range. Plates were exposed to blue light (470 nm) for 60 min and further incubated in the dark. After incubation at 37 °C for 24 h, the minimum inhibitory concentrations were determined by measuring absorbance at 625 nm using a plate reader (Epoch Microplate Spectrophotometer, BioTek Instruments, Inc., USA).

UV-Vis spectra of p-GQDs, AuNPs, GQDs-EDA, GQDs-TA, and GQDs-EDA-AuNPs are presented in Fig. 1. All GQD spectra show a strong absorption in the UV part of the spectrum (between 200 nm and 350 nm) and tails extending into the visible region. The main absorption band in the p-GQD spectrum is located at 200 nm and assigned to the π→π* transition of aromatic CC bonds. In GQDs-EDA spectrum, a new band at 270 nm is assigned to stretched delocalization of π-electrons over the N functional groups [43], and the one at 222 nm is associated with an n→π* transition involving functional groups with electron pairs, O or N [44,45]. GQDs-TA and GQDs-EDA-AuNPs spectra (Fig. 1a, blue and red curves) show the same bands, indicating the presence of sp2 domains, N-functional groups, and CO groups. In the GQDs-EDA-AuNPs spectrum, an additional, low-intensity band at 530 nm was noticed and enlarged in Fig. 1b. This band is consistent with the surface plasmon resonance (SPR) of AuNPs and proves their presence in the GQDs-EDA-AuNPs composite [46].

Figure S1 (Supporting Information) shows FTIR spectra of p-GQDs, GQDs-EDA, GQDs-TA, and GQDs-EDA-AuNPs. In the p-GQD spectrum, the following bands are detected: at 3430 cm−1 from O-H bonds, at 2973 cm−1, 2928 cm−1, and 2869 cm−1 from the C-H bonds in -CH/-CH2 groups, at 1711 cm−1 from CO bonds in carboxyl groups, at 1579 cm−1 from the CC bonds in π-conjugated domains, at 1339 cm−1 from C-OH bonds, and 1089 cm−1 from C-O bonds in the epoxy functional group. For the GQD-EDA, the changes were noticed (curve 2, Figure S1, Supporting Information): a new band at 3219 cm−1 from N-H bonds in amino groups [47]; bands at 2673 cm−1, 2582 cm−1, 2518 cm−1, and 2445 cm−1 assigned to C-H bonds in EDA fragments [48], and C-N bonds in the amino group [49]; band at 1643 cm−1 from CO bonds in amide groups [50], at 1700 cm−1 weak band is assigned to asymmetric stretching of the amide I band [51]; new bands at 1511 cm−1 and 1446 cm−1 originate from the N-H bending of primary amine [47] and C-N bending in the amide group formed in the reaction [52] while those at 1256 cm−1 and 819 cm−1 corresponds to N-CO [53] and N-H [54], respectively. In GQDs-EDA, new functional groups are identified: primary amino groups, amide groups, and CH2 groups.

In the GQDs-TA spectrum (curve 3, Figure S1, Supporting Information) the band at 3417 cm−1 was shifted to 3386 cm−1 due to functionalization with 3-amino-1,2,4-triazole. The band at 1636 cm−1 stems from CO bonds in amide groups established between the amino group of triazole and carboxyl groups of GQDs [50]. The band at 1700 cm−1 is observed, while new bands at 1531 and 1455 cm−1 are assigned to the CC bonds of aromatic domains in the triazole ring [55], while at 1223 cm−1 is associated with N-N stretching vibrations in the same ring [56].

In the GQDs-EDA-AuNPs spectrum (curve 4, Figure S1, Supporting Information), 3368 and 1090 cm−1 bands were shifted due to modification of the chemical environment while those at 3219, 1511, and 819 cm−1 disappeared indicating the binding of the AuNP's carboxyl group with amino groups of GQDs-EDA. The absence of bands from 2673 to 2058 cm−1 in the GQDs-EDA-AuNPs spectrum is due to the bonding of AuNPs to EDA fragments on the GQDs-EDA surface.

Survey XPS spectra are shown in Figure S2 (Supporting Information), and display C 1 s (ca. 285 eV) and O 1 s (ca. 532 eV), N 1 s regions (ca. 400 eV) for all samples, and, the Au 4 f doublet around 85 eV for the GQDs-EDA-AuNPs. Table 1 reports the relative abundance of elements. The most abundant element is C, whereas O denotes the existence of O-functional groups. Detected N proves the N-groups are incorporated. A relatively low Au content is measured.

The high-resolution analysis for the GQDs-TA, GQDs-EDA, and GQD-s-EDA-AuNPs samples is presented in Fig. 2 for C1s and N1s and in Figure S3 (Supporting Information) for O1s and Au 4 f. For all samples, the C 1 s region showed the presence of graphitic carbon, as evidenced by the asymmetric line shape. Other peaks refer to C−O/C−N (286.0 eV), CO (287.5 eV), and O−CO/N−CO (288.8 eV). However, it was not possible to separate the aminic contribution from the alcoholic contribution, nor the carboxylic from the amidic. The presence of organic, both aminic and amidic N (399.7 eV) is indirectly confirmed by the analysis of the N 1 s region. It additionally reveals a trace amount of protonated nitrogen (401.7 eV). The O 1 s region analysis (Figure S3, Supporting Information) shows the presence of at least two species, the less abundant one attributable to the presence of adsorbed water, while the peak at ≈ 532 eV is typical of both amidic oxygen and aromatic carboxylic group; in this case, too is not possible to separate the two contributions.

In the Au 4 f region spectrum, it was observed that 85.31 % of total Au was reduced from Au(III) (doublet 85.9 eV, 89.57 eV) to Au(0) doublet 84.0 eV, 87.63 eV), demonstrating decoration of GQDs-EDA with AuNPs through the coupling reaction (Figure S3, c, Supporting Information). The asymmetric lineshape of the resulting data fit suggests the Au(0) is present in aggregates large enough to show an extended electronic delocalization. The XPS data concerning aromatic C, aminic, amidic, and carboxylic groups in modified dots agree with FTIR results.

The chemical composition of p-GQDs and modified ones was investigated using FE-SEM with an energy-dispersive X-ray detector (EDX, Fig. 3). All spectra show the presence of carbon and oxygen signals, with the largest abundance (Fig. 3). The highest wt% and at% of N were detected in GQDs-EDA (16.50 wt%). Measured Au (at% 0.39) was similar to XPS data (0.41 at%). The spatial distribution of elements was analyzed and homogeneity could be observed in EDS maps (Figure S4, Supporting Information). The EDS results are in agreement with XPS and FTIR analyses.

To estimate the amount of the attached EDA/TA/AuNPs attached to GQDs, at% of N and Au obtained by XPS were converted into wt%. The ratio between the total molar mass of the N and the molar mass of the attached functional group was used to estimate the wt% of the attached groups (Table S1, Supporting Information). For GQD-EDA it was calculated that EDA is 42.27 wt% in GQD-EDA, for TA was obtained 26.64 wt%, and in GQDs-EDA-AuNPs, wt% 30.28 is from EDA and 5.51 wt% are AuNPs.

Fig. 4 shows the PL spectra of GQDs-EDA, GQDs-EDA-AuNPs, and GQDs-TA, while p-GQDs and AuNPs are shown in Figure S5 (Supporting Information). All samples show excitation-depended PL emission (Table S2, Supporting Information). With the excitation wavelength increase (λexc), the center of PL emission spectra is red-shifted. All modified GQDs show the highest PL intensity when excited at 380 nm while p-GQD at λexc of 300 nm. AuNPs showed low-intensity emission bands (Figure S5, Supporting Information).

Photoluminescence of GQDs remains one of the most attractive features of GQDs along with multicolor PL emission [57–59]. The exact origin of photoluminescence is still an open question along with the excitation-dependent behavior [59]. Amine groups contribute to surface states [60] and reduce the non-radiative recombination caused by COOH and epoxy groups [61], which explains the high intensity of the emission bands in all three modified dots. Excitation-depended PL of GQDs was explained by differences in surface and internal structures, and it is related to surface states as well as the mechanisms of charge and energy transfers [62].

The higher PL intensity of GQDs-EDA-AuNPs compared to AuNPs and p-GQDs (Figure S5, Supporting Information) is assigned to localized surface plasmon (LSP) resonance-enhanced PL, which originates from a localized enhanced electric field [63] and plasmon resonance energy transfer [64]. Visible light excites the electrons of metal NPs and has energy above the Fermi level of GQDs [65].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology analysis of modified GQDs: DLS, zeta sizer, AFM
ce:section.ce:para: According to DLS particle size distribution (Figure S6, a-d), the largest diameter was measured in the GQDs-EDA-AuNPs sample where an average diameter was 76 nm, for GQDs-EDA was 52 nm, while in the GQDs-TA sample was measured 45 nm. For p-GQDs, an average diameter was 14 nm. With the increase in the average particle size in all modified GQDs, it is suggested that EDA molecules were bound to the COOH of two GQDs creating the bridge between two or more GQDs. This led to the formation of larger particles in the GQDs-EDA samples (52 nm). When this sample was involved in an EDCI-catalyzed reaction with AuNPs, the part of free amino groups created amide bonds with COOH of AuNPs. Due to a further increase in the particle size (76 nm), it is suggested that AuNPs reacted with amino groups from several different GQDs-EDA particles which led to an enlargement of the total particle diameter.

The surface charge of p-GQDs was measured to be −34.3 ±5.5 mV, +11.4 ± 0.8 mV for GQDs-EDA, −31.5 ± 1.0 mV for GQD-TA, and ζ-potential of the GQDs-EDA-AuNPs was −25.6 ± 2.8 mV. In GQDs-EDA, the surface charge indicates the presence of NH2 groups. In the case of GQDs-TA, the charge was similar to p-GQDs (-31.5 mV). The pKa values of 1,2,4-triazole are 2.50 and 9.89, while at pH 7 this ring is neutral [66]. Thus, the charge of GQDs-TA was from COOH groups that were not involved in amide bonds.

AFM imaging combined with viscoelastic and electrostatic force microscopy was used (
Figs. 5 and 6). In Fig. 5(a), a representative AFM image of AuNPs is shown, where only oval particles could be observed, while the phase mode image (Fig. 5, b) revealed that average AuNPs are 14.2 ± 3.1 nm. In Fig. 5c, the viscoelastic AFM image of AuNPs shows a Young modulus of elasticity in the 4.83–6.23 GPa range. In the case of GQDs-EDA-AuNPs, AFM imaging (Fig. 5, d) shows that GQDs were organized into larger particles while in the center of these particles are the highest. Phase mode (Fig. 5, e), shows the inner substructure of the GQDs-EDA-AuNPs complex, indicating that GQDs are covered with small particles as cycled in Fig. 5, e, and concluded that those are AuNPs. Analysis of elastic properties showed that GQDs-EDA-AuNPs complex particles are much softer than AuNPs, with Young modulus in the −126 to −109 MPa range.
This analysis provided significant conclusions about changes in the mechanical properties of the AuNPs and proved the formation of the complex with GQDs.

Surface charge distribution at the nanoscale level was studied using electrostatic force imaging (Fig. 6, b, and d). In the case of GQDs (Fig. 6b), considering that dots are observed as a lighter area than support, they are mostly electron deficient. Fig. 6d shows that the GQDs-EDA-AuNPs complex has a negative charge, as evidenced by a darker region compared to support. Similar results were obtained when graphene oxide was reduced to graphene and this transformation was directly monitored using EFM mapping [67].

During the reaction of EDCI coupling, GQDs formed amide bonds with amino groups of EDA at one end, while the second amino group of EDA remained free ( Scheme 1). XPS and FTIR analyses showed the presence of amino and amide groups in the GQDs-EDA sample, while the total charge of the particles was shifted from −34.3 mV to +11.4 mV. During the reaction, COOH groups from AuNPs were bonded to free amino groups of the GQDs-EDA. In the case of TA, amide bonds were created, while triazole rings were present on the GQD surface as displayed in Scheme 1.

During the EDCI coupling reaction, GQDs formed amide bonds with EDA amino groups at one end, while the second amino group remained free (Scheme 1). Microscopic analysis proved that AuNPs were positioned at the center of the GQDs-EDA-AuNP complex nanomaterials, serving as the nucleus of the structure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of photo-generated ROS production: ABDA and DPBF tests
ce:section.ce:para: The 1O2 photo-generating process by GQD samples was also determined using a method based on the singlet oxygen sensor ABDA. In the presence of 1O2 generated by photosensitizer, ABDA rapidly and quantitatively converts to a steady-state endoperoxide product. The reaction is monitored by the decrease of absorbance intensity at 420 nm in the presence of the photosensitizer. Fig. 7 displays UV-Vis spectra of ABDA recorded upon blue-light illumination without (a), and in the presence of the GQDs-EDA (b), GQDs-EDA-AuNPs (c), and GQDs-TA (d). The ABDA test results for p-GQDs are presented in Figure S7 (Supporting Information). Significant dye photodegradation was not detected even after 270 min of irradiation (Fig. 7a). GQDs-EDA led to complete degradation of ABDA after 80 min, GQDs-EDA-AuNPs after 150 min, and GQDs-TA after 270 min of illumination (Fig. 7b, c, and d, respectively). Nevertheless, the main ABDA absorption band is present even after 300 min of irradiation in the presence of p-GQDs. The obtained results prove the ability of GQD samples to produce 1O2 upon blue light.

Additionally, DPBF was used as a capturing agent for singlet oxygen. In the presence of 1O2, the absorption band of DPBF at 410 nm is reduced due to the endoperoxide formation via a [4+2] cycloaddition [40] proportionally to endoperoxide abundance in solution [68]. In Figure S8 (Supporting Information), absorbance spectra of the DPBF mixed with p-GQDs are presented while in Figure S9 are spectra of DPBF (a) and DPBF mixtures with GQDs-EDA (b), GQDs-EDA-AuNPs (c), and GQDs-TA (d) are shown. The peak at 410 nm decreased with time illumination. Total DPBF degradation is noticeable in the sample of GQDs-TA, after 15 min, while p-GQDs degraded DPBF in 10 min.

Both ABDA and DPBF assays confirmed that GQDs modified with EDA, TA, and AuNPs produce singlet oxygen under blue light which was a motive for investigating the effects of these particles on several bacterial strains using the same irradiation conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photoinduced antibacterial activity of modified GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The antibacterial activity of modified GQDs was investigated against 8 bacterial strains. Water dispersions were prepared in a starting concentration of 2 mg mL−1 and used to prepare acquired dilutions. After applying the selected concentrations, bacterial strains were exposed to blue light irradiation (470 nm) for 60 min. In these experimental conditions, we found that the minimum inhibitory concentrations (MIC) were lowest when using colloid GQDs-EDA-AuNPs against the following bacterial strains: K. pneumoniae (7.81 µg mL−1), S. aureus (7.81 µg mL−1), MRSA (3.9 µg mL−1), and E. faecalis (3.9 µg mL−1) (Table S3, Supporting Information). In contrast, colloid GQDs-TA did not exhibit any observable MIC concentrations against any of the tested bacterial strains. The MIC concentrations for the GQDs-EDA sample fell in the range of 125 µg mL−1 to 500 µg mL−1 when tested against various bacterial strains. This can be declared as medium antibacterial activity.
Growth inhibition was noticed compared to treatment control for the GQDs-EDA-AuNP sample against L. monocytogenes, E. coli, A. baumani, and P. aeruginosa and results are presented in Fig. 8.

These results prove that the GQDs-EDA-AuNPs sample is a potent antibacterial agent against K. pneumoniae, S. aureus, MRSA, and E. faecalis in combination with blue light illumination. On the other hand, GQDs-EDA showed a mild antibacterial efficiency, while GQDs-TA did not show any effect on selected bacterial strains.

In Table 2. the comparison between MIC values obtained in our work and the literature values is presented. The MIC values in our work are much lower than previously reported. That indicated the effectiveness of GQDs-EDA-AuNPs as an antibacterial agent.

Following exposure to modified GQDs and blue light, only GQDs-EDA-AuNPs exhibited a high level of effectiveness against 4 bacterial strains. However, spectroscopic analysis of singlet oxygen production revealed a substantial generation of 1O2 from all three modified GQDs (Figs. 7 and 8). AuNPs enhance the production of reactive oxygen species (ROS) when combined with PS [76–78]. Gold nanoparticles have a higher extinction coefficient than a classical photosensitizer but to generate singlet oxygen, O2 molecules must be adsorbed on their surface to provide a rapid energy transfer between the two [79]. In the case of GQDs-EDA-AuNPs, the planar complex ( Scheme 2) allowed direct access of O2 molecules to gold nanoparticles, and energy transfer from localized surface plasmon resonance to O2 is possible. On the other hand, GQDs-EDA produces singlet oxygen during photoexcitation through the energy transfer of excited PS to O2 molecules [80]. According to Fig. 9, the kinetics of ABDA decomposition are similar for GQDs-EDA-AuNPs and GQDs-EDA, while GQDs-TA induced slower singlet oxygen production. Thus, slow ROS production may be a reason for the lack of antibacterial effect of GQDs-TA (Fig. 8). In the case of GQDs-EDA, where singlet oxygen is produced faster (Fig. 9), mild antibacterial activity was detected against MRSA and K. pneumoniae. While the diagram of ABDA decomposition shows results similar to GQDs-EDA, outstanding antibacterial activity, particularly towards MRSA of the GQD-EDA-AuNP sample could be explained by synergistic effects of AuNPs and GQDs singlet oxygen production from both.

The structure of GQDs was modified in the EDCI coupling reaction with two amines, EDA and TA, and AuNPs, to improve the photo-induced antibacterial activity of starting particles. Although positively charged dots possess better antibacterial effects, GQDs-EDA showed only effects towards MRSA. Triazole-containing compounds showed promising effects on various pathogens, while in combination with GQDs effects on bacterial strains were not detected. Nevertheless, GQDs-EDA-AuNPs showed astonishing effects against several bacterial strains: K. pneumoniae (7.81 µg mL−1), S. aureus (7.81 µg mL−1), MRSA (3.9 µg mL−1), and E. faecalis (3.9 µg mL−1). Investigation of photophysical properties, the efficiency of singlet oxygen generation, and antibacterial activity showed that in GQDs-EDA-AuNPs, the main role plays AuNPs. These nanoparticles improved the photogeneration of 1O2 under visible light and the bactericidal effect of GQDs-EDA-AuNPs.
